title: Matrimony
html:
Big Question

Can the love between a man and a woman become a sacrament of God's own love?

Louis Martin was a bachelor watchmaker who thought he would never marry. Zélie Guérin wanted to be a nun, but the superior told her she had no vocation. God had other plans. They met on a bridge in Alençon, France. He looked at her and sensed an inner voice: «This is the one I have prepared for you.» They married. They had nine children; four died young. The five daughters who survived all entered the convent. One of them was St. Thérèse of Lisieux, Doctor of the Church. Louis and Zélie were not a perfect couple —they faced hardships, illness, grief— but they lived their love in God and from God. Today they are canonized saints: the first spouses beatified together —not as individuals but as a married couple. Their story poses a question that runs through all of Scripture: can human love —concrete, daily, sometimes weary— reflect divine love? The Church's answer is astonishing: not only can it, but Christ elevated it to a sacrament. Christian marriage is not a civil contract or a cultural tradition. It is a visible sign of Christ's invisible love for his Church. And it begins, like every story of salvation, «from the beginning.»

«From the Beginning»: Marriage in God's Plan

When Jesus speaks about marriage, he does not begin by citing Moses or the prophets. He turns his gaze back to the origin: «Have you not read that he who made them from the beginning made them male and female?» (Mt 19:4). Jesus goes to the root: to God's original plan, inscribed in creation itself (CCC 1603-1605).

Genesis reveals it in language rich with meaning. God creates man in his image and likeness, and creates him as a communion of persons: «male and female he created them» (Gen 1:27). The image of God is not reflected in the isolated individual but in the communion of love between man and woman. «It is not good that the man should be alone» (Gen 2:18). God himself declares that solitude is not the original plan. And after a parade of animals where Adam finds no «helper fit for him,» God forms the woman from his side. Adam's reaction is a song of love: «This at last is bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh!» (Gen 2:23). The first poem of humanity is spoken by a man to his wife (CCC 1605).

And the text concludes: «Therefore a man shall leave his father and his mother and cling to his wife, and they shall become one flesh» (Gen 2:24). Three verbs that define marriage: leaving (new freedom), clinging (exclusive covenant), becoming one flesh (total communion, bodily and spiritual). Jesus will quote exactly this verse when revealing the indissoluble heart of marriage (CCC 1605).

The vocation to marriage is inscribed in the very nature of the human being, created in the image of the God who is Love (1 Jn 4:8). This is why marriage is not a cultural invention or a historical development; it is part of creation's original design. The complementarity of man and woman, the call to communion, openness to fruitfulness: all of this is already there «in the beginning» (CCC 1603).

Activity

Read Genesis 1:27-28 and 2:18-25 aloud. Then take a piece of paper and a pencil and draw two circles slowly moving toward each other until they overlap, forming a shared space. In the shared space write: «God.» In each circle write your name and the name of someone you love (spouse, boyfriend/girlfriend, friend). Reflect: where is God in that relationship? Is he at the center of the shared space or on the margins? Ask him to take the center.

Under the Regime of Sin: The Wound and God's Pedagogy

The original plan was beautiful. But sin entered and damaged every relationship, including marriage. «Because of you the ground is cursed» (Gen 3:17): the harmony between man and woman is wounded. Where there was once communion of persons, domination emerges: «He shall rule over you» (Gen 3:16). Love twists into possession; complementarity becomes a power struggle (CCC 1606-1608).

Throughout the Old Testament, Israel developed marriage legislation that reflected both the hardness of the human heart and God's patient pedagogy. Practices such as polygamy —the patriarchs had several wives— and divorce, which Moses permitted due to the people's hardness of heart (Deut 24:1), appear. But alongside these elements, which Jesus would call a temporary concession, a whole theology of spousal love also flourished (CCC 1609-1611).

The prophets —especially Hosea, Jeremiah, Isaiah, and Ezekiel— use marriage as the great metaphor for the covenant between God and Israel. God is the faithful Bridegroom; Israel the unfaithful bride who abandons him for other gods. And yet God does not break the covenant. Hosea, called to marry Gomer —a woman who would be unfaithful to him— and to take her back, embodies in his very life God's unconditional love for his people. The image of spousal love between Yahweh and Israel prepares the ground for understanding that human marriage is called to be far more than a contract (CCC 1611).

The Song of Songs, the most daring love poem in all of Scripture, sings of the passion between bridegroom and bride as a reflection of divine love. Jewish and Christian tradition has always read it theologically: God loves his people with a bridegroom's love. This divine pedagogy prepared humanity for the moment when the definitive Bridegroom —Christ— would wed humanity forever (CCC 1611).

Connection

Read Hosea 1-3 (the prophet's marriage to Gomer) and Ephesians 5:25-33 (Christ the bridegroom of the Church). The Old Testament shows the God-Bridegroom who forgives infidelity. The New Testament shows Christ as the Bridegroom who loves to the point of giving his life. What does this connection reveal about the nature of Christian marriage? Have you experienced God's faithfulness even in moments of distance?

Marriage in the Lord: Christ Elevates It to a Sacrament

With the coming of Christ, marriage takes a radical leap. Jesus does not merely restore the original plan of Genesis; he elevates it. Marriage between the baptized becomes a sacrament: an efficacious sign of grace that configures the spouses to the mystery of the covenant between Christ and the Church (CCC 1612-1617).

The decisive moment is the dialogue with the Pharisees about divorce. They ask Jesus: «Is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife for any cause?» They expected Jesus to take sides among the rabbinical schools of the day. But Jesus does not engage in casuistry. He sends them back to the origin: «Have you not read that he who made them from the beginning made them male and female, and said, 'Therefore a man shall leave his father and his mother and cling to his wife, and the two shall become one flesh'? So they are no longer two but one flesh. What therefore God has joined together, let not man separate» (Mt 19:4-6). Jesus abrogates the Mosaic concession of divorce and restores the indissolubility of marriage «from the beginning» (CCC 1614).

The disciples are stunned and reply: «If such is the case of a man with his wife, it is better not to marry» (Mt 19:10). Jesus does not lower the standard. He confirms indissolubility, and adds that some renounce marriage «for the sake of the kingdom of heaven» (Mt 19:12): consecrated virginity and sacramental marriage are not in competition; they illuminate each other. Both are vocations to total, lifelong love (CCC 1620).

St. Paul develops this theology with extraordinary density in Ephesians 5, a text proclaimed at countless Catholic weddings: «Husbands, love your wives, as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her» (Eph 5:25). The love of a Christian husband is an image of Christ's sacrificial love. «This mystery is profound —and I am applying it to Christ and the Church» (Eph 5:32). The Greek word Paul uses is mysterion, which the Latin Vulgate translates as sacramentum. Here is the biblical root of sacramental language about marriage: it is mystery, it is sacrament, it is a real sign of Christ's love for his Church (CCC 1616).

Context

Jesus' teaching on the indissolubility of marriage was one of the most countercultural parts of his ministry. In his time, male divorce was an almost absolute right; it was enough to find «something indecent» in the wife (Deut 24:1) and the husband could divorce her. Jesus declares that Moses made that concession «because of the hardness of your hearts» (Mt 19:8). The early Church guarded this teaching as something distinctive to the Gospel. The first Christians astonished the pagan world precisely by their marital fidelity. What does this tell us about the mission of Christian marriages today?

The Wedding at Cana: The First Miracle at a Wedding

John's Gospel narrates Jesus' first sign in a very specific context: a wedding. At Cana in Galilee, Jesus, his mother, and his disciples are invited to a wedding celebration. During the feast, the wine runs out. Mary intercedes: «They have no wine.» Jesus replies that his «hour» has not yet come. But Mary tells the servants: «Do whatever he tells you» (Jn 2:1-5). Jesus turns water into wine —and not just any wine, but the best wine— (CCC 1613).

The Church has always seen in Cana the confirmation of the goodness of marriage and the announcement of its elevation to sacramental dignity. Christ's presence sanctifies the human reality of marriage. The wedding at Cana is the «theological place» where it is revealed that Jesus blesses the conjugal union. «The Church attaches great importance to the presence of Christ at the wedding at Cana. She sees in it the confirmation of the goodness of marriage and the proclamation that henceforth marriage will be an efficacious sign of Christ's presence» (CCC 1613).

The detail is beautiful: Jesus' first public miracle is not a healing or a multiplication of loaves. It is saving a wedding feast, turning the water of ritual purification into the wine of messianic joy. Since Cana, Christian marriage carries within it the joy of the Gospel. Where there is a Christian marriage, there is also «new wine»: Christ is present, celebrating, blessing, transforming.

The Four Pillars of Christian Marriage

The Catechism identifies the essential properties of Christian marriage: unity, indissolubility, and fidelity. To these is added openness to life as an essential good of conjugal love. They are like four pillars holding up the edifice of sacramental marriage (CCC 1644-1654).

Unity. Marriage is the union of one man and one woman in an exclusive covenant. «They are no longer two but one flesh» (Mt 19:6). This unity excludes every form of polygamy —both polygyny and polyandry— and demands integral communion: of bodies, hearts, projects, spiritual life. Conjugal love is total: it embraces the whole person (CCC 1644-1645).

Indissolubility. «What God has joined together, let no man put asunder» (Mt 19:6). The marriage bond is perpetual. This is not a question of human endurance but of sacramental grace: God himself unites the spouses with a bond that does not break —not even through civil separation or, in some cases, separation of bed and board. Civil divorce does not dissolve sacramental marriage. The Church does not recognize divorce as a rupture of the bond (CCC 1646-1651).

Fidelity. From the indissoluble character of marriage flows fidelity —exclusivity in love— which the spouses promise to each other on their wedding day. Fidelity is a reflection of God's unbreakable faithfulness to his people. Adultery, throughout Scripture, is not a «mere infidelity»; it is a rupture of the sacramental sign, a profanation of the temple that is the other's body (CCC 1646).

Openness to life. Conjugal love is ordered by its very nature to the procreation and education of children. «Be fruitful and multiply» (Gen 1:28): fruitfulness is a blessing, not a burden. Spouses cooperate with God's creative love. The Catechism teaches that every conjugal act must remain open to the transmission of life (CCC 1652-1654). This grounds the Church's teaching on birth regulation: natural methods respect the language of the body; artificial contraception breaks the unity between love and fruitfulness (Humanae Vitae, 1968). The Church is not «against birth control»; she proposes natural family planning, which respects the integrity of the conjugal act and strengthens dialogue between spouses.

Going Deeper

Read Pope St. Paul VI's encyclical Humanae Vitae (1968). It is a prophetic document that predicted the consequences of widespread contraception: loss of respect for women, trivialization of sexuality, state imposition of anti-natalist policies. Its positive proposal —natural methods of recognizing fertility— has been validated by modern science (sympto-thermal method, Billings method). Why do you think the Church considers that separating love from life impoverishes conjugal love?

Matrimonial Consent: The Spouses Are the Ministers

One of the most unique features of Matrimony among all the sacraments is who administers it. It is not the priest or bishop. The ministers of the sacrament are the contracting parties themselves —the man and woman who are marrying—. The priest or deacon is a qualified witness on behalf of the Church, but the sacrament is brought about by the free consent of the spouses (CCC 1623, 1625-1631).

What is matrimonial consent? The Catechism defines it precisely: «Matrimonial consent is an act of the will by which a man and a woman mutually give and accept one another through an irrevocable covenant in order to establish marriage» (CCC 1626). It is not a feeling, even if it is normally accompanied by feelings. It is an act of free will: I want to give myself to you and receive you forever, exclusively, open to life.

This is why marriage is invalid if freedom is lacking (due to coercion or grave fear), if capacity is lacking (due to impediments such as impotence or a prior bond), or if any of the essential goods of marriage are intentionally excluded: unity, indissolubility, fidelity, openness to life (CCC 1628).

Consent is exchanged publicly before the Church. The ordinary canonical form requires that a Catholic marry before a priest (or deacon) and two witnesses, following the liturgical rite. But what is essential —the sacrament itself— is accomplished by the spouses as they pronounce their vows. In the Eastern Churches, the presiding priest who blesses is also considered a minister of the sacrament together with the spouses (CCC 1623).

Activity

Look up the formula of matrimonial consent (found in the Missal, in the liturgy of Matrimony). Read it slowly. The spouses say: «I, N., take you, N., to be my wife/husband. I promise to be faithful to you, in good times and in bad, in sickness and in health, to love you and to honor you all the days of my life.» Every word carries weight. Every word is spoken before God. Which word of this formula do you find most demanding? Which most beautiful?

Priests in Their Home: The Domestic Church

There is an expression that appears in the earliest Christian centuries and which the Second Vatican Council recovered with vigor: the family is ecclesia domestica, the «domestic church» (Lumen Gentium 11; CCC 1655-1658).

This means that the Christian home is not merely a place of daily living. It is a sacred space where faith is lived, where prayer happens, where the Gospel is handed on from parents to children, where forgiveness and service are learned. Before parishes and cathedrals exist, the first «church» a child knows is his or her own house: bedtime prayers, grace at meals, the crucifix on the wall, the image of Our Lady in the living room.

In the domestic church, parents exercise a true baptismal priesthood over their children: they teach them to pray, speak to them about God, bring them to the sacraments. They are the first catechists. And the mutual love between the spouses —with its gestures of tenderness, patience, daily forgiveness— is a silent but extremely powerful preaching. St. John Chrysostom called the family «the little Church.»

The fourth commandment («honor your father and your mother») illuminates the whole life of the domestic church. Children learn to honor, to obey, and one day —when parents grow old— to return in care what they received. The Christian family is a school of human and supernatural virtues: generosity, patience, justice, respect for others (CCC 1657).

Mixed Marriages and Disparity of Cult

The Church addresses with pastoral realism the reality of marriages between a Catholic and a baptized non-Catholic —mixed marriage— or between a Catholic and a non-baptized person —disparity of cult— (CCC 1633-1637).

In both cases, the marriage can be valid, but it requires a special license from the bishop (dispensation). The Catholic party commits to doing everything possible to have the children raised in the Catholic faith, while respecting the conscience of the spouse. The Church recognizes that these marriages can be occasions for growth in ecumenical and interreligious dialogue, but also warns of the objective difficulties they entail: divergences on the Eucharist, on methods of birth regulation, on the religious education of children. It is not a question of tolerance but of truth in love (CCC 1634).

A fundamental principle: what unites is shared faith; what separates doctrinally cannot be ignored. This is why preparation for mixed marriage requires honesty and pastoral accompaniment. Many mixed marriages are beautiful witnesses of how grace also works amid difference.

Contemporary Challenges: A Pastoral Approach

Marriage and the family face challenges today that were almost unimaginable a hundred years ago. The Church, faithful to the Gospel but always close to human suffering —like Christ with the Samaritan woman— addresses these realities with doctrinal truth and heartfelt mercy (CCC 1638-1654).

Divorce. The Church upholds Christ's teaching on indissolubility. She does not recognize divorce as a dissolution of the marriage bond. Nevertheless, she is conscious that many people live through situations of rupture —sometimes through no fault of their own— and offers them pastoral accompaniment. Annulment is a canonical process that examines whether the marriage was actually valid from its celebration (due to lack of freedom, simulation of consent, etc.). Pope Francis' Amoris Laetitia (2016) encourages accompanying, discerning, and integrating people in situations of family fragility (CCC 1649-1651).

Cohabitation before marriage. Many couples live together before marrying —sometimes for economic reasons, sometimes by conviction—. The Church teaches that sexual relations belong within the context of marriage, where love is protected by a definitive commitment. Cohabitation does not allow one to live the total gift of sexuality in the truth of the body's language: «I give myself totally to you,» but «if it doesn't work out, each to one's own.» Pre-marital pastoral care invites couples to rediscover the value of chastity as a path of freedom and mutual respect (CCC 1632).

Same-sex unions. The Church teaches that persons with same-sex attraction «must be accepted with respect, compassion, and sensitivity. Every sign of unjust discrimination in their regard should be avoided» (CCC 2358). But she cannot recognize as marriage what does not correspond to God's design inscribed in nature: the spousal complementarity of man and woman. The Church distinguishes between the person —always worthy of welcome— and the claim to equate same-sex unions with marriage, which by its nature requires sexual duality. As the Congregation for the Doctrine of the Faith declared: «There are absolutely no grounds for considering homosexual unions to be in any way similar or even remotely analogous to God's plan for marriage and the family» (2003). The pastoral response never excludes the person, yet always proclaims the truth that sets free.

Biblical Connection: Love That Does Not Pass Away

«Husbands, love your wives, as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word, so that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, that she might be holy and without blemish. In the same way husbands should love their wives as their own bodies. He who loves his wife loves himself. For no one ever hated his own flesh, but nourishes and cherishes it, just as Christ does the church, because we are members of his body. 'Therefore a man shall leave his father and mother and cling to his wife, and the two shall become one flesh.' This mystery is profound, and I am applying it to Christ and the church.» Ephesians 5:25-32

This text is probably the deepest passage in all of Scripture concerning marriage. St. Paul begins with an apparently practical exhortation —«husbands, love your wives»— and in four verses unfolds a theology that spans the entire history of salvation. The love of the Christian husband is an image of Christ's sacrificial love: a love that gives to the uttermost («gave himself up»), a love that purifies («that he might sanctify her»), a love that beautifies («without spot or wrinkle»). Paul weaves Gen 2:24 —the original formula for marriage— together with the mystery of Christ and the Church. What was a natural union becomes elevated to a supernatural sign. And he concludes: «This mystery is profound.» The Greek word mysterion is not a metaphor. It is the sacramental key: marriage participates really —not symbolically— in the mystery of Christ's covenant with the Church (CCC 1616).

For Reflection

  1. Genesis says: «It is not good that the man should be alone» (Gen 2:18). Have you ever experienced that solitude is not God's plan? How does companionship —from a spouse, friends, the Christian community— help you reflect the communion of love God desires?
  2. Jesus speaks clearly about indissolubility: «What God has joined together, let no man separate» (Mt 19:6). In a world where so many things are provisional and disposable, what does love «forever» mean to you? Is it frightening or hopeful?
  3. The four pillars of marriage —unity, indissolubility, fidelity, openness to life— are not only for spouses. They also illuminate other relationships: how do you live fidelity in your friendships? How do you live fruitfulness —giving life to others— in your own circumstances?
  4. St. Paul says that the husband's love should be like Christ's: «he gave himself up» (Eph 5:25). True love involves sacrifice. Is there someone for whom you are called to give yourself —maybe wearying yourself, maybe receiving nothing in return? Are you doing it?
  5. Louis and Zélie Martin, the parents of St. Thérèse, were not perfect but let themselves be transformed by grace. If you could ask God one thing for the families around you —your own, your friends'— what would you ask?

Key Takeaway

Key Takeaway: Christian marriage is not a blessed human contract: it is a sacrament —an efficacious sign of grace— that configures the spouses to the irrevocable covenant of Christ and the Church, unites them in one flesh forever, and sends them into the world as visible witnesses of God's faithful, fruitful, and indissoluble love for humanity.

Cultural Resources

Saints Louis and Zélie Martin — Beatified together in 2008 and canonized by Pope Francis in 2015, they are the first spouses raised to the altars as a married couple, not as individuals. Their story is a testimony that holiness can be lived in marriage: raising children, working (he a watchmaker; she with a lace-making workshop), enduring illness (Zélie died of breast cancer at 45; Louis suffered a degenerative neurological disease). Five daughters entered the Carmelites, among them St. Thérèse of the Child Jesus, Doctor of the Church. Their memorial is celebrated on July 12. Their tomb in Lisieux receives pilgrims from all over the world who ask their intercession for families.

St. Joseph and the Virgin Mary — The marriage par excellence in the history of salvation. It was a virginal marriage —Mary conceived by the power of the Holy Spirit— but a real one, with everything that implies: commitment, tenderness, fidelity, mutual care, protection. St. Joseph was called «son of David» (Mt 1:20), a title that places him in the messianic genealogy. His yes to Mary —in a situation that was humanly inexplicable— was an immense act of faith. Today, contemplating Joseph and Mary is to contemplate marriage lived in the fullness of grace.

Mexican Wedding Traditions — Mexican culture has enriched the celebration of marriage with symbols of deep Christian significance. The arras (coins): thirteen coins that the groom gives to the bride as a sign that they will share everything —material goods and responsibilities—. The lazo (lasso): a cord in the shape of the number eight (the number of the eternal covenant) that joins the spouses by the shoulders during the consecration, symbolizing that «they are no longer two but one flesh.» The bouquet to Our Lady: the bride lays her bouquet before an image of Mary, asking for her intercession. These are traditions that speak to the heart and catechize through beauty.

«The Marriage of the Virgin» by Raphael — Painted in 1504 (Pinacoteca di Brera, Milan), this Renaissance masterpiece depicts the marriage of Mary and Joseph. At the center, the high priest of the temple joins the hands of the spouses. Joseph carries a flowering staff —the divine sign that he was the chosen one—. In the background, a circular temple (Bramante's influence) symbolizes divine perfection. The geometric composition, luminous colors, and the dignity of the figures convey that marriage is not a worldly event: it is a sacred action carried out before God and before the people.

Film: «Fireproof» — This 2008 film (starring Kirk Cameron) tells the story of Caleb, a firefighter who saves lives at work but whose marriage is collapsing. His father proposes a challenge: «The Love Dare,» 40 days of concrete acts of service to his wife, even if she does not respond. Inspired by Christian values, the film is a parable about what it truly means to love: not to feel, but to do; not to wait, but to give first. It has helped countless struggling marriages and is a common resource in Catholic marriage retreats.
